ocr.text: The following table presents a comprehensive overview of re-interventions conducted on patients over an eight-year period
from 2001 to 2008, highlighting trends in the frequency of these interventions categorized by life stage.
Re-interventions (n) in the same patient  2001  2002  2003  2004  2005  2006  2007  2008  AAPC (95%CI)2
3 re-interventions in the same year  27  56  132  166  220  240  290  295  +27.5 (24.4; 30.7)
>3 re-interventions in the same year  0  0  7  3  17  16  15  24  +45.9 (29.9; 63.9)
Life Stage  Seed  Seedling  Plantlet  Juvenile  Adult
Seedling  0.02  0.06  205
Plantlet  0.05  4.83  13.7
Adult  0.26
% correct C  Copy number error (median)  Purity error (median)
m  THetA  THetA  THetA  CNAnorm  ABSOLUTE
Specifically, the data reveals that the total number of re-interventions in the same patient decreased from 3,268 in 2001 to 3,039
in 2008, indicating a downward trend in the necessity for repeat procedures. This decline prompts further examination of the
underlying factors contributing to this phenomenon, particularly in the context of patient outcomes and treatment efficacy
across different life stages, such as Seed, Seedling, Plantlet, and Juvenile. Additionally, the Age-Adjusted Percentage Change
(AAPC) with a confidence interval of 95% indicates a significant shift in re-intervention patterns, underscoring the importance
of longitudinal data in assessing the effectiveness of medical interventions over time. Understanding these trends not only aids
in improving patient management strategies but also provides insight into the efficacy of initial treatments and the overall
healthcare delivery system. The implications of these findings will be critical for healthcare practitioners and policymakers
aiming to enhance patient care and optimize resource allocation in clinical settings.
